coredata.dc:title: Water resistance, thermal stability, luminescence enhancement of core-double shell structure K2TiF6:Mn4+ phosphor
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yuelan
Yu, Yan
Zhong, Xue
Lu, Chunyan
Wu, Dongmei
Liao, Sen
Huang, Yingheng
Zhang, Huaxin
coredata.dc:description: In order to improve fluorescent thermal stability and water-proof of Mn4+-doped red emitting fluoride phosphors applied for white light emitting diodes (WLEDs), in this work, a coating strategy is explored to prepare graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and K2TiF6 double coated Mn4+ doped K2TiF6 red emitting phosphor with high efficiency, fluorescent thermal stability and water-proof. After coating with double shell of GQDs and K2TiF6, fluorescent intensity, fluorescent thermal stability and water-proof are remarkably improved. The mechanism of the above improvements is explained and suggested. The results implied that double coating by GQDs and matrix is an effectively way to obtain Mn4+-doped fluoride phosphors with high emission, fluorescent thermal stability and water-proof at the same time.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Optical composite materials
Fluorescent thermal stability
Water-proof
Double coating
Red emitting phosphor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Luminescent properties at different mn/Ti molar ratios
Determination the optimal concentration of GQDs
Analyses of the optimal K2TiF6 concentrations

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns, FT-IR, XPS and high resolution XPS of Mn 2p of the three samples, (i) KTF:0.04Mn4+, (ii) KTF: 0.04Mn4+@GQDs 4 mg/mol, (iii) KTF:0.04Mn4+@GQDs 4 mg/mol @0.43KTF, (v) NH3-GQDs: (a) XRD patterns, (b) FT-IR spectra, (c) XPS energy spectra (d), (e), (f) High resolution XPS of Mn 2p.
SEM images and the corresponding EDS spectra of (i) KTF:0.04Mn4+, (ii) KTF:0.04Mn4+@GQDs 4 mg/mol and (iii) KTF:0.04Mn4+@GQDs 4 mg/mol @0.43KTF: (a), (b), (c) SEM images, (d), (e), (f) EDS spectra.

Luminescent properties and UV–Vis absorption spectra of samples (i)-(iv), (i)KTF:0.04Mn4+, (ii)KTF: 0.04Mn4+@GQDs 4 mg/mol, (iii) KTF:0.04Mn4+@GQDs 4 mg/mol@0.43KTF and (iv) KTF@GQDs 4 mg/mol: (a), (b) Comparing PLE and PL spectra, (c) CIE chromaticity diagram of sample (iii), (d), (e), (f) decay curves and (g) UV–Vis absorption spectra.
PLE and PL spectra of K2TiF6:Mn4+ with different molar ratio of K2MnF6/K2TiF6: (a) PLE, (b) PL.
PLE and PL spectra of K2TiF6:0.04Mn4+@GQDs ymg/mol: (a) PLE, (b) PL.
PLE and PL spectra of KTF:0.04Mn4+@GQDs 4 mg/mol @zK2TiF6: (a) PLE, (b) PL.

Water resistance test of three sample: (i)KTF:0.04Mn4+, (ii)KTF: 0.04Mn4+@GQDs4mg/mol and (iii) KTF:0.04Mn4+@GQDs4mg/mol@0.43KTF: (a) luminescent photographs excited by 365 nm UV light after soaking in water, (b)，(c) photographs under natural light, (d) PL integrated intensities with different soaking time in water.

Thermal performances of samples (i)-(iii): (a), (b), (c) PL spectra of sample (i), (ii), (iii), (d) Integrated intensity curve, (e) Integrated intensity curves of stoke and anti-stoke peaks, (f) chromaticity shift of sample (iii), (g) CIE color coordinates of sample (iii), (h) E
a curves, (i) TG curve of sample (iii): (i)KTF:0.04Mn4+, (ii)KTF: 0.04Mn4+@ GQDs4mg/mol and (iii) KTF:0.04Mn4+@GQDs 4 mg/mol @0.43KTF

Performances of prototype WLEDs, using the mixing phosphors of (iii)KTF:0.04Mn4+@ GQDs 4 mg/mol@0.43KTF and YAG:Ce3+ based on InGaN chip under a 20 mA drive current: (a) Electroluminescence spectra, (b) CIE chromaticity diagram, (c) Luminescence photos and (d) Durability test.
ICP element analysis results of samples (i-iii).
Negative thermal quenching effect of some samples.

row.entry: Integrated emission intensity and corresponding temperature

head.ce:title: Water resistance, thermal stability, luminescence enhancement of core-double shell structure K2TiF6:Mn4+ phosphor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning, Guangxi, 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Environment and Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning, Guangxi, 530004, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning, Guangxi, 530004, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning, Guangxi, 530004, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In order to improve fluorescent thermal stability and water-proof of Mn4+-doped red emitting fluoride phosphors applied for white light emitting diodes (WLEDs), in this work, a coating strategy is explored to prepare graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and K2TiF6 double coated Mn4+ doped K2TiF6 red emitting phosphor with high efficiency, fluorescent thermal stability and water-proof. After coating with double shell of GQDs and K2TiF6, fluorescent intensity, fluorescent thermal stability and water-proof are remarkably improved. The mechanism of the above improvements is explained and suggested. The results implied that double coating by GQDs and matrix is an effectively way to obtain Mn4+-doped fluoride phosphors with high emission, fluorescent thermal stability and water-proof at the same time.

Furthermore, it is interesting that Liu et al. [45] used double coating strategy to synthesize K2TiF6:Mn4+@OA@SiO2 (OA: hydrophobic oleic acid), which water resistance was significantly improved. Therefore, the double coating strategy is a promising strategy. Unfortunately, whether fluorescent thermal stability and water resistance can be improved synchronously through the coating strategy is rarely reported. Inspired by the double coating strategy reported by Liu et al. [48], in this paper, based on our previous work [9] of K2SiF6:Mn4+@GQDs, a coating strategy is applied to synthesize double coating samples of K2TiF6:Mn4+@GQDs@K2TiF6. Some multiple enhancing effects inducing by the double coating layers were occurred, which fluorescent thermal stability, water resistance as well as emission intensity were simultaneously improved.

ce:section.ce:para: The world's demand for high-quality light and backlit devices has become increasingly strong. Red emitting phosphors with high CRI (color rendering index) and low CCT (correlated colour temperature) are an important component for WLEDs. Among many emitting red phosphors, Mn4+-doped K2TiF6 are one of the most potential one for WLEDs [1–29]. The phosphor has five red narrow-emission bandwidth peaks and one strong broad blue excitation peak.

However, the phosphor has two shortcomings that need to be addressed: (a) Its water resistance is poor. In the high moist environment, [MnF6]2- in the phosphors is easy hydrolyzed into compounds of oxides and hydroxides, which leads to the emission intensity to loss rapidly [1–3]. (b) Its fluorescent thermal stability is not high. Under the operating temperature of LED chip, their luminescent intensity tends to drop to 60–80% of that at room temperature [30].

For the sake of overcoming the above two problems, a lot of attempts have been tried. On one hand, some authors use strategies of special phase structures [10–12] and the surface coating to enhance the water resistance [1–3,31–44]: (a) The reported phase structure strategies include the application of metastable stable phases and the application of special central ions. For example, the intrinsic water resistance of K2GeF6:Mn4+ is very poor because it will automatically transform from hexagonal phase to trigonal phase in HF solution. But, Han et al. [10] reported when some of Ge (iv) ions were substituted by Si (iv) ions, the metastable hexagonal phase of K2(Ge1-x Si x )F6:Mn4+ was stable in HF solution, and resulted in good water resistance. Furthermore, Zhang et al. [11] reported K3GaF6:Mn4+ prepared with hydrous K2MnF6 and K2GaF5(H2O) also had good water resistance. Finally, Wu et al. [12] reported that Cs2NbOF5:Mn4+ had intrinsic good water resistance, because Nb5+ could play a major role to self-suppress the hydrolysis of [MnF6]2− groups. (b) In strategies of the surface coating [1–3,31–44], the coating materials include organic compounds and inorganic insoluble substances. For example, organic compounds such as poly propylene glycol [14], oleic acid [17], octadecyltrimethoxysilane [18] and alkyl phosphate [42] were used to apply as the coating layer to enhance the water resistance. However, the luminescence of the phosphors can be markedly quenched by organic compounds. Besides, organic coating layers are possibly degraded at severe environments. On the contrary, coating of inorganic compounds has also confirmed to be an effective strategy to improve the moisture-proof property. For example, inorganic substances such as nanoscale carbon [39], CaF2 [43], Al2O3 [41,44] and TiO2 [44] were also applied as the coating layer to enhance the water resistance of the phosphors, which were attributed to heterogeneous coating.

Nevertheless, crystal defect defects at the interface between shell and core often formed after heterogeneous coating, thus decreasing luminous quantum yields of phosphors. In contrast, the inorganic homogeneous shell coating has no such disadvantages. Examples of inorganic homogeneous shell coating are as follows: (a) Wang et al. [1] and Peng et al. [2] coated K2SiF6:Mn4+ by using K2SiF6 layer to improve water resistance. (b) Chen et al. [3] used K2TiF6 shell to improve water resistance of K2TiF6:Mn4+. (c) Peng et al. [10] and Liu et al. [15] coated surfaces of Rb2SnF6:Mn4+ [13] and K2GeF6:Mn4+ [15] with rare-Mn4+ layer formed from in situ reduction, and resulted in significantly enhancement of moisture-resistant.

On the other hand, fluorescent thermal stability can be improved by negative thermal quenching effect [4,6–10,45–48]. For example, Zhang et al. [30] synthesized α-K3AlF6: Mn4+, which had good thermal stability at 150–180 °C due to the effect. Ye group [8] obtained CsMoO2F3:Mn4+ with the effect, which significantly improved thermal stability at 150–180 °C. Ye group [48] also reported that K2TiF6:Mn4+ had the effect and resulted in good thermal stability at 120–133 °C. In addition, in our previous work [9], we also got K2SiF6:Mn4+@GQDs with the effect, which obviously increased thermal stability at 150–180 °C [9]. However, most Mn4+-doped fluoride phosphors have poor fluorescent thermal stability, and the thermal quenching occurs at 125 °C or even 100 °C [8].

Please refer to the supplementary materials for the content of the experiment. Properties of NH3-contained graphene quantum dots (NH3-GQDs) are shown in Table S1 and Figs. S1-S6 (Supplementary Materials).

The results of ICP are shown in Table 1 , which molecular formulae of the samples are KTF:0.04Mn4+ (i), KTF:0.04Mn4+@GQDs 4 mg/mol (ii) and KTF:0.04Mn4+@GQDs 4 mg/mol@0.43KTF (iii), respectively. The results show that K2TiF6:0.04Mn4+@GQDs 4 mg/mol has been successfully coated by K2TiF6 in the sample (iii). Fig. 1 a shows the XRD patterns of samples (i-iii) ((i) KTF:0.04Mn4+, (ii) KTF:0.04Mn4+@ GQDs 4 mg/mol, (iii) KTF:0.04Mn4+@GQDs 4 mg/mol@0.43KTF). Indexed results of Fig. 1 are listed in Table 2 , which match with standard data of K2TiF6 (PDF#73–2110) (Table 2). Fig. 1a depicts that the three samples have no any impurity peaks, indicating that they are pure phase of K2TiF6.

However, the order of crystal plane preferential growth is different in the three samples. The orders of the first and second preferential growth planes are as follows: (a) 101 > 021 for the sample (i), (b) 021 > 101 for the sample (ii), (c) 101 > 002 for the sample (iii). The results show that different preferential growth planes can be induced by different synthesis conditions.

Fig. 1b describes FT-IR spectra of samples (i-iii) and NH3-GQDs. The GQDs shows its characteristic peak at 1636 cm−1 (C = C vibration). In Fig. 1b, samples (i)-(iii) have a peak at 1636 cm−1, but samples (i) and (iii) are a weak one and sample (ii) is a strong one. The former can be attributed to the C = O vibration peak of CO2 adsorbed by the sample in air, while the latter can be attributed to overlapping peaks of the C = C vibration peak of GQDs and the C = O vibration peak of CO2, indicating that GQDs is coated on the sample (ii). To further verify that K2TiF6 was coated on the surface of the sample (iii), XPS was used to investigate the surface elements of the sample (i-iii). Fig. 1c is the XPS energy spectra of the samples (i-iii). The results show that the three samples have elements of Mn, Ti, K, F, C and O, which C and O come from GQDs, adsorbed CO2 and H2O, respectively. Fig. 1d–f is the high resolution XPS for Mn 2p. In Fig. 1d–f, there is the signal of Mn 2p for the samples (i-ii), while no signal of Mn 2p to be detected in sample (iii), indicating that its surface is coated by KTF.

Fig. 2 shows the EDS spectra and SEM images of samples (i-iii). Fig. 2a–c describe that the three samples have smooth and irregular particles with a size of about 20–50 μm. A obviously stratified surface of the sample (iii) indicates that the surface is covered by K2TiF6 via layer epitaxial growth of KTF on KTF:0.04Mn4+@GQDs 4 mg/mol (Fig. 2c). Fig. 2d–f shows that elements of the three samples are as follows: (a) the sample (i) has K, Ti, Mn and F; (b) the sample (ii) has C, K, Si, Mn and F, indicating that its surface is coated by GQDs; (c) the sample (iii) has K, Si and F, but C and Mn cannot be detected because its surface is coated with KTF, and results in the absence of GQDs and K2MnF6. Obviously, the results of Fig. 2f show that the surface of sample (iii) has been coated by KTF, which is supported by the results of FTIR, XPS and SEM. In conclusion, it is not difficult to find that GQDs and KTF have been coated on the sample (iii) in turn.

In order to explore the surface coating structures of the samples (ii-iii), the two samples were characterized by using HRTEM. In view of the very large size of the particles in the two samples, the necessary and light grinding treatment was carried out before HRTEM characterization. HRTEM images of the samples (ii-iii) are shown in Fig. S7. Figs. S7(a-c) are HRTEM images of the samples (ii). These three images clearly show that the surface of the sample (ii) is covered with particles smaller than 10 nm, which they are particles of GQDs. Figs. S7(d-f) are HRTEM images of the samples (iii). The three images definitely depict that the sample (iii) has a shell structure. This shell is a matrix layer formed after coating of KTF. The results indicate that the sample (iii) has core-double shell structure, which one shell is GQDS layer, and the another shell is KTF layer.

Fig. 3 is luminescent spectra and UV–Vis absorption spectra of the samples (i-iv): (i) KTF:0.04Mn4+, (ii) KTF:0.04Mn4+@ GQDs 4 mg/mol, (iii) KTF:0.04Mn4+@GQDs 4 mg/mol @0.43KTF, (iv) KTF@GQDs 4 mg/mol. Fig. 3 shows that the sample (iv) does not emit light, while the other three samples emit light. Fig. 3a–b shows that the samples (i)-(iii) have normal excitation and emission bands [1–14,45–50].

Coating effects of GQDs and K2TiF6 in Fig. 3a–b are summarized as: (a) After coating of GQDs, the excitation and emission intensities of the sample (ii) are all greatly enhanced to 2.36 times those of the sample (i). The results are similar to those previously reported by our group [9]. (b) After coating of K2TiF6, the excitation and emission intensities of the sample (iii) are also increased to 2.46 and 1.04 times those of the samples (i-ii), respectively. The enhanced results are verified by Chen et al. report [3].

Reasons for the results are that: (a) Due to their conjugated large π bond, GQDs can coordinate with Mn4+, and enhances emission of Mn4+ through donating electron pair to Mn4+ [9]. (b) Coating layer of K2TiF6 cuts off the way of energy transfer to the surface defects, so the emission intensity is improved [3].

Based on the fact that GQDs is directly used as the starting material in this paper (Supplementary materials), combining the results of Fig. 1b–c, Fig. 2e and Fig. 3a–b with the Supplementary materials, it can further be confirmed that GQDs has been successfully coated on the sample (ii).

From the results of UV-VIS spectra in Fig. 3g, it can be found: (a) the intensities of the sample (i)-(iii) are different, but the wavelengths of the absorption peaks are unchanged; (b) the intensity of the sample (ii) (GQDs coated sample) was much stronger than that of the sample (i) (uncoated sample), but the intensity of the sample (iii) (KTF and GQDs double coated sample) was only slightly stronger than that of the sample (ii) (GQDs single coated sample). The above results are consistent with those of PLE spectra in Fig. 3a–b.

The chromaticity coordinates (CC) of the three samples are calculated based on Fig. 3a–b, and the results are shown in Fig. 3c, and they are (0.6916, 0.3083), (0.6921, 0.3077) and (0.6920, 0.3079) for the samples (i-iii), respectively. Fig. 3c shows that three chromaticity coordinate points almost overlap, illustrating that three samples have the same red color.

Fig. 3e–f are lifetime curves of the samples (i-iii). The results are τ (i) = 5.67, τ (ii) = 5.69 and τ (iii) = 5.72 ms, respectively. Lifetime order of the three samples is τ (iii) > τ (ii) > τ (i), indicating that each coating layer can increase lifetime, but the increase is not obvious.

The QY
i of the samples (i-iii) are calculated with Eq. (S1), and are 87.61, 97.77 and 99.93%, respectively. The QY
i order of the three samples is QY
i (iii) > QY
i (ii) » QY
i (i), indicating that each coating layer can increase QY
i , but the most significant increase was contributed by GQDs coating.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescent properties at different mn/Ti molar ratios
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 is luminescent properties of samples at different Mn/Ti molar ratios (x). Fig. 4a–b shows that curves of excitation and emission intensities are parabolas. When x = 4%, the two curves have their maximum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination the optimal concentration of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on KTF:0.04Mn4+, samples of K2TiF6:0.04Mn4+@GQDs y
mg/mol were synthesized. The luminescent intensities of the samples at different concentration (y) of GQDs (NH3-GQDs) were explored, and presented in Fig. 5 . Fig. 5 reveals that the emission intensity curve at different y is a bell-shaped curve, and the highest point of the curve is at y = 4 mg/mol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analyses of the optimal K2TiF6 concentrations
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 shows luminescent intensities of the samples at different z (z = K2TiF6/K2TiF6:0.04Mn4+ molar ratios). Fig. 6 shows that curve of the emission intensity is a parabola-like curve, and its highest point is at z = 1.00.

Fig. 7 is water resistant of the samples (i-iii). Fig. 7(a1-a3) show that after soaking in water 300 min, luminescent intensities of the three samples are all decreased, which order of the intensity is (iii) > (ii) > (i). Fig. 7b–c indicate that in appearance, the color of the samples has darkened, and the order of darkening is (i) > (ii) > (iii).

Fig. 7d shows PL integrated intensities with different soaking time in water. It can be seen from Fig. 7d that: (a) When t = 0, intensities of (i)-(iii) are all 100%. (b) When t = 5 min, the intensities of (i) - (iii) are sharply decreased to 47.8%, 66.3% and 96.0%, respectively. (c) Then, after t = 50–250 min, the intensities of (i) - (iii) decreased slowly and linearly. (d) When t > 250 min, intensity curves of (i)-(iii) become three straight lines which are basically parallel to the abscissa, indicating that the intensities of (i) - (iii) remain basically unchanged. (e) When t = 300 min, intensities of (i)-(iii) decreased to 39.2, 46.8 and 90.6%, respectively. The results indicate that the water resistance of the samples was also improved to a certain extent after coating GQDs. This may be due to the fact that GQDs have a very large surface area, and means that the coated the sample has less [ MnF6]2- to contact with water. That is that less [ MnF6]2- is hydrolyzable, and therefore the water resistance is improved.

The corresponding reasons for the results of Fig. 7d can be attributed to: (a) After soaking in water, Mn4+ on surfaces of the samples will soon hydrolyze into black brown opaque MnO2 or Mn(OH)4, so the intensities decrease sharply. (b) There is no coating on the surface of the sample (i), the sample (ii) has a coating of GQDs, and the sample (iii) has two coating layers of GQDs and KTF. So the order of Mn4+ concentration on the sample surfaces is: (i) > (ii) »(iii). The greater the Mn4+ on the sample surface, the greater the amount of hydrolysis product (MnO2 or Mn(OH)4). Therefore, the decreasing order of the intensities is: (i) > (ii) > > (iii). (c) After t > 250 min, with the increase of soaking time, on the one hand, the Mn4+ on the sample surface will be consumed, on the other hand, layer of the hydrolysis products (MnO2 or Mn (OH)4) deposited on the sample surface will also prevent further hydrolysis, Therefore, the intensities decrease very slowly, or even remain basically unchanged.

Chen et al. [3] reported that K2TiF6:Mn4+@K2TiF6 was obtained by coating K2TiF6 with cation exchange method. The results show that the water resistance of the coated samples is improved obviously. Details are as follows: After soaking in water for 300 min, the emission intensity of the uncoated sample (K2TiF6:Mn4+) is only about 10% of the original value, while the emission intensity of the coated sample (K2TiF6:Mn4+@K2TiF6) increases to about 60% of the initial value [3].

The result of Fig. 7d shows that the sample (iii) has good water resistance, which is better than that of K2TiF6:Mn4+@K2TiF6 prepared by Chen et al. [3]. The reason may be that the former is double coated with GQDs and K2TiF6, and the latter is only single coated with K2TiF6, and the ability of double coating to improve water resistance is greater than that of single coating.

Fig. 8 shows luminescent thermal properties of the samples (i-iii) at varying temperatures. Fig. 8a–c indicate that integrated emission intensities of the samples are obviously impacted by temperature, but the shape and position of PL peaks are not obviously influenced by temperature. Curves of integrated emission intensities of the three samples at varying temperatures are shown in Fig. 8d. The curves are all parabola curves, showing that there are negative thermal quenching effects for the three samples. However, the strengths of the effect are different, and the order of the strengths are (iii) > (ii) > (i). The effect is new that has not been reported in previous works of K2SiF6:Mn4+@K2SiF6 [1,2], K2TiF6:Mn4+@K2TiF6 [3]. The ratios of integrated emission intensity at 150 °C to that initial value at 30 °C are as follows: (a) 95.7% for the sample (i), (b) 173.9% for the sample (ii), (c) 187.9% for the sample (iii). So, the order of the effect is (iii) > (ii) » (i). The results of Fig. 8d show that: (a) The effect of the uncoated sample is weak, which is agreement with that result of K2TiF6:Mn4+ reported by Hou et al. [48]. As a result, its thermal stability at 150 °C is not high enough. (b) The coating of GQDs greatly enhances the effect, making the sample have a high thermal stability in the range of 150–180 °C; (c) The second coating of KTF further enhances the effect, and the enhancement amplitude is relatively small. It is clear that the enhancement of the effect is mainly induced by the GQDs coating.

Integrated intensity curves of anti-Stokes and Stokes peaks of samples (i-iii) change at various temperatures are shown in Fig. 8e, which are all parabola curves. Fig. 8e shows that every W
a curve is high than its corresponding W s curve, indicating that with the increase of temperature, the W
a and W s also increase. But the former is faster than the latter. These negative thermal quenching phenomena are described with Eqs. (S2, S3) [51].

The equations indicate that not only the W a and W s would enhance with the increase of temperature, but also the W a will enhance more fastly than W
s with the increasing temperature. Since the working temperature of WLEDs chip is as high as 150 °C [4], luminescent thermal stability of phosphor is a crucial performance for high-power WLEDs applications. To obtain red -emitting phosphors with high thermal stability under 150 °C, a plenty of work has been carried out [4,6–8,45–48,52].

It is interesting to learn that the thermal stability of some Mn4+-doped phosphors can improved by the negative thermal quenching effect [4,6–8,45–48,52]. For example, α-K3AlF6:Mn4+ [30], K2SiF6:xMn4+ [6], K2SiF6:Mn4+ [7], CsMoO2F3:Mn4+ [8], K2SiF6:Mn4+@GQDs [9] and K2TiF6:Mn4+ [43] are the phosphors with the negative thermal quenching effect. The negative thermal quenching effects of them and the samples (i)-(iii) are shown in Table 3 .

It can be learned from Table 3 that the samples (ii)-(iii) have good luminescent thermal stability. Results of Figs. 7d and 8d indicate that the thermal stability and the water resistance of K2TiF6:Mn4+ can be effectively improved by the double-coating of graphene quantum dots and K2TiF6. The cause for high negative-thermal-quenching effect of the sample (iii) may belong to support of the double-coated shells of GQDs and K2TiF6. It is confirmed that both the radiative transition probabilities (RTP) and the nonradiative transition probabilities (NTP) of Mn4+ are increased with the rising of temperature [5,30]. So, when the former increases much faster than that of the latter with the rising of temperature, the effect is occurred. Hence, mechanism for the effect of the sample (iii) may be explained as following: (a) On the one hand, due to their conjugated large π bond, after coating of GQDs, GQDs will coordinate with Mn4+, and donates electron pair to Mn4+ [9], then greatly induces the RTP larger than that of the NTP at high temperature, which is verified by the curve (ii) of Fig. 8d. (b) On the other hand, coating layer of K2TiF6 will cuts off the energy transfer way to the defects of surface, which is confirmed by curve (iii) of Fig. 8d. Under support of the K2TiF6 shell, the RTP will be larger than that of the NTP at high temperature. Then, integrated PL intensity is increased with the rising of temperature [3], which is confirmed by curve (iii) of Fig. 8d.

In our previous work [53], we found that K2SIF6: Mn4+@K2SiF6 has a negative thermal quenching effect. So, in order to find out which factor makes the main contribution to the effect of the sample (iii), thermal performance of KTF: 0.04Mn4+@KTF is studied and shown in Fig. S8. Fig. S8 (a) is PL spectra of the sample, which the PL intensities are strongly influenced by temperature. Integrated intensity curves of four samples ((i)KTF:0.04Mn4+, (ib)KTF:0.04Mn4+@ KTF, (ii)KTF: 0.04Mn4+@ GQDs4mg/mol and (iii) KTF:0.04Mn4+@GQDs 4 mg/mol @0.43KTF) is described in Fig. S8(b). Fig. S8(b) shows that the height order of the four curves is as follows: (iii) > (ii) > > (ib) > (i), indicating that for the effect of the sample (iii), coating of GQDs made a major contribution, while coating of KTF made a much smaller contribution. In addition, the results of Fig. S8 (b) confirm that the sample (iii) is indeed double-coated by GQDs and KTF.

Thermal stability durability test of the sample (iii) at different temperatures (150, 160 and 180 °C) were also studied. The results are given in Fig. S9. Fig. S9 shown that first, the integrated intesnisty curves of the three temperatures are all straight lines, which slightly decrease with the increasing of time. The results of Fig. S8 further suggest that the sample (iii) has good thermal stability durability at different temperatures (150, 160 and 180 °C).

Chromaticity shifts (ΔE) of the sample (iii) at various temperatures are calculated with Eq. (S4) [54–57] and the results are described in Fig. 8f. Fig. 8f shows ΔE curve at different temperatures for the sample (iii). The curve is a nonlinear curve. Fig. 8f shows that ΔE increases gradually with the increase of temperature. From Fig. 8f, value of ΔE at 175 °C is 27.70 × 10−3, which is better than that of CSASNE (44 × 10−3 at 175 °C) [55].

CIE color coordinates of the sample (iii) at different temperatures are shown in Fig. 8g. Fig. 8g shows that there is a slight shift of color coordinates with the increase of temperature, which is attributed to the slight broadening of the sharp red emission peaks with the increase of temperature. The results also show that the sample (iii) has good color stability.

Activation energy (E
a ) of thermal quenching for the samples (i-iii) is calculated with Eqs. (S5-S6). The calculated results are shown in Fig. 8h, which the E
α of the samples (i-iii) are 0.5298, 0.5982 and 0.6233 eV, respectively. It can be learned that E
α is obviously enhanced by coating of GQDs. TG curve of the sample (iii) is shown in Fig. 8i.

The TG below 900 °C presents in two steps and mass loss starts at about 90 °C. It is clear that the mass loss is about 0.166% at 200 °C, which is attributed to the loss of adsorbed water. The result of Fig. 8i shows that thermal stability of the sample (iii) is high.

Prototype WLEDs are obtained by coating epoxy resin mixing of phosphors on blue emitting InGaN chip. Here, the phosphors include yellow emitting YAG:Ce3+ and red emitting phosphor (the sample (iii)). A 20 mA drive current is used to test the WLEDs. The test results are shown in Fig. 9 . Spectrum of the WLEDs is shown Fig. 9a. The (CC) of the WLEDs are obtained basin on spectrum data of Fig. 9a. The results are (0.3790, 0.3580) (Fig. 9b), suggesting that white light of the WLEDs is warm one. Luminescent photo of WLEDs (Fig. 9c) also indicates the WLEDs emit a warm white light (luminescence efficiency = 101.3 lm W−1, CCT = 3826 K, R
a = 90.2). Durability test of the WLEDs is depicted in Fig. 9d. Fig. 9d shows that when time>5 h, CCT and R
a remain basically unchanged, indicating durabilities of CCT and R
a are good. So, the sample (iii) can be used to improve properties of high power WLEDs.

In summary, a series of K2TiF6:0.04Mn4+@GQDs@K2TiF6 red emitting phosphors have been synthesized by a coating strategy. The formation of GQDs and K2TiF6 double shells can greatly improve the luminescent performances of the sample: (a) The emission intensity is 90.69% of the initial emission intensity after soaking 300 min in water, implying that water resistance of the sample is good. (b) The integrated emission intensity at 150 °C is 187.9% of that the initial value at 30 °C, suggesting that luminescent thermal stability of the sample is good. (c) Both QY
i and luminescent intensity are greatly improved, which QY
i is 99.93%, and the PL intensity is 2.46 times that of the uncoated sample. Furthermore, the warm prototype WLEDs fabricated by using the sample (iii) as red emitting component exhibit warm white light (luminescence efficiency = 101.3 lm W−1, CCT = 3826 K, R
a = 90.2) under the 20 mA drive current. The results show that the sample (iii) (KTF:0.04Mn4+@GQDs 4 mg/mol@0.43KTF) has great commercial application prospect for blue light-based high power WLEDs.

Yuelan Li: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Yan Yu:Investigation. Xue Zhong: Investigation. Chunyan Lu: Investigation. Dongmei Wu: Investigation. Sen Liao: Conceptualization, Supervision. Yingheng Huang: review&editing, Visualization. Jinqiao Long: review&editing, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21661006 and No. 21965004), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Zhuang Autonomous Region, China (Grant No. 2019GXNSFDA245022), the innovation Project of Guangxi Graduate Education (Grant No. YCSW2020015), the Students Experimental Skills and Innovation Ability Training Fund Project of Guangxi University (No. 202010593186).
